entry.title: Smarter Document Management in SAP Cloud ALM: Introducing Document Versioning
entry.content:
Effective documentation control is essential for traceability and compliance. As processes evolve and content changes, users need a clear understanding of document history — what changed, when, and by whom.

To support this, we’re introducing Document Versioning in SAP Cloud ALM.

This new feature ensures that every document change is tracked, every version is identifiable, and every released state is preserved in a structured, transparent way.

Key Highlights

New Attribute: “Version”

A new attribute, “Version”, is now available under the Additional Information tab.

  • Default value upon document creation: 1
  • The field is display-only for end users.
  • The version number updates automatically based on document status changes.

When a document is first created or directly set to Released, its version is 1.
If a released document is reopened (status changed from Released to In Progress), the version number increments by one.

“Create new version” Button: Your Path to Improvement

Sometimes, even finalized documents need substantive updates. Rather than allowing direct editing (which would compromise audit integrity), SAP Cloud ALM provides the "Create new version" button.

When you click "Create new version" on a locked, released document:

  • A new version of the document is automatically created
  • The new version becomes editable with all fields unlocked
  • The original final version remains archived and unchanged in display mode only
  • Complete traceability is maintained between versions
  • Your audit trail shows exactly when and why the document was reworked

This mechanism ensures that:

     ✅   You never lose the original finalized content
     ✅   Changes are traceable to specific rework instances
     ✅   Stakeholders can see the evolution of document thinking
     ✅   Compliance requirements remain satisfied 

Automatic Version Handling and History

Each version is tracked historically, allowing users to reference older iterations whenever needed.

  • The version number becomes part of the document history, ensuring visibility across lifecycle changes.
  • Each document version shows its own history, including previous document versions. The entire document history is always displayed on the latest version.
  • Users can configure a Version column in document overview tables.
  • Each version receives a unique technical identifier, enabling direct access to the version’s content.

This ensures that document tracking remains auditable and intuitive across teams and projects.

Once you navigate to older version, the information will get visible on the user interface. New button "View Latest Version" is accessible and leads to the most current version. 

Locking of document content on Released status

In enterprise document management, protecting finalized documents from unintended changes is essential—yet teams still need the flexibility to manage relationships and assignments. SAP Cloud ALM meets this need with an intelligent document-locking mechanism that safeguards content while still allowing targeted, strategic updates.

Finalizing a document marks a critical milestone in any compliance- or quality-driven workflow. Once a document reaches its final status, stakeholders must be confident that its content will remain unchanged. At the same time, real-world processes demand the ability to update relationships and adjust assignments. The answer isn’t a rigid lock—it’s a smart one.

What Gets Locked When a Document Reaches Final Status?

When you set a document to final status, SAP Cloud ALM protects the core document content from any modifications. Protected Elements (Read-Only) are:

  • Document Title — Prevents accidental or unauthorized name changes
  • Content Field — Locks the main document body
  • External File Tab — Protects attached files and prevents replacements
  • Additional Information — Secures metadata and supplementary details
  • Reference Tab — Maintains the integrity of internal references

This comprehensive protection ensures that the definitive version of your document remains exactly as approved—critical for regulatory compliance, audit trails, and change management.

What Remains Editable: Strategic Flexibility

Recognizing that document management doesn't end at finalization, SAP Cloud ALM deliberately excludes the Relations tab from locking. using button "Edit (limited)" the Relations tab remains fully editable, allowing teams to:

  • Link documents to new requirements or change requests
  • Connect to newly created dependent items
  • Update cross-document relationships as your project evolves
  • Maintain traceability without creating new document versions

 

Implementation Best Practices

Train Users on the Distinction
Educate teams that "locked" doesn't mean "immutable"—it means "protected unless formally reworked." Help them understand when to use Relations vs. Rework.

Use “Create new version” Strategically
Reserve the “Create new version” button for substantive changes. Use Relations updates for linking and contextual adjustments.

Monitor Rework Frequency
Track which documents generate frequent reworks. This pattern may indicate premature finalization or unclear requirements.

Leverage Assignment Updates
Use the Assignments feature to keep document stewardship current without creating unnecessary versions.

Conclusion

By combining intelligent content locking with strategic versioning and relationship management, this feature ensures that your documents remain both protected and adaptable. Finalized content stays secure and auditable, while teams retain the agility to respond to evolving business needs—whether through targeted relationship updates, assignment adjustments, or formal rework when substantive changes are required.

The result is a document management approach that satisfies compliance demands without sacrificing operational reality. Your audit trails remain complete, your stakeholders stay confident in document integrity, and your teams can work efficiently within a framework designed for both governance and growth.

Implement these practices, educate your teams on the distinction between locking and versioning, and you'll find that effective documentation control becomes less of a constraint and more of a strategic asset.

entry.title: Re: Planning Strategy 50 - PIR Consumption Behavior & SFG...
entry.content:
Hello

The fact that you don't have a PIR for the whole sales order quantity should not prevent you to produce a material and the significance of the PIR in planning strategy 50 is to plan the components ahead of time. If you have components in stock, you will be able to start the final assembly as soon as you receive a sales order. 

You don't need to maintain the planning strategy for SFG or components. You should use the field Individual/Collective in the material master to define if a component will behave like the finished product and wait for the SO to start procurement, or if procurement should start ahead of time.

Regards,
Caetano

entry.title: Electronic Batch Record (EBR) in SAP Digital Manufacturing
entry.content:
For manufacturers in regulated industries, closing a batch should be fast, transparent, and compliant—without chasing paper. The new Electronic Batch Record (EBR) capability in SAP Digital Manufacturing consolidates execution data and supporting documents into a single document-based record framework, guided by an e-signature–based workflow and complete audit trail. The result is a released, immutable electronic record that supports customers toward meeting regulatory requirements for electronic batch records and can be used to support downstream batch disposition.

Why this matters?

  • Confidence for regulated industries: Support toward compliance with industry regulations through controlled workflows, electronic signatures, and complete audit trails.
  • Faster, cleaner batch close: Ability to consolidate shop-floor data in document-based framework reduces manual effort and rework.
  • Clear accountability:  A role-based workflow supported by e-signatures and a tamper-evident audit trail.
  • Better downstream decisions: A released EBR supports efficient, confident batch disposition.

How EBR fits into your end-to-end process

Before the EBR process starts, SAP Digital Manufacturing helps you execute with discipline and traceability, ensuring the right data is captured at the right time:

  • Production Preparation (for example Process Engineer): Build the digital foundation with a Master Batch Record (MBR) by preparing work instructions, data collection parameters, tolerances, and CPP/CQA definitions, using master recipes and routings replicated from SAP S/4HANA. This ensures the digital Master Batch Record is ready for execution.
  • Execution (for example Operators and Supervisors): Execute the batch using POD2.0. The system automatically captures data either via Operator input in the POD or real time through shopfloor integration such as SFC start/completion events, material consumption and genealogy, confirmations, data collections, nonconformances (NCs), and any required e-signatures using the e-signature plugin. Supervisors and QA reviewers use review-by-exception to assess NCs designated as EBR-relevant—verifying details, adding comments, approving or rejecting deviations, and tracking status history with e-signatures.

With preparation and execution complete, the EBR process begins—collecting all relevant information and routing it through an e-signature–based workflow to create a document-based batch record with a full audit trail.

 

The EBR lifecycle at a glance

  • Create the Draft EBR (f.e. by Supervisor)
    • Automated initiation of the EBR using the EBR Create API.
    • Assemble the record by attaching required documents (for example, work instructions, product history reports, and nonconformance documentation).
    • The EBR is editable only while in Draft. Once complete, the Supervisor submits the draft with an electronic signature. The signature is logged in the Activity Log and the record becomes non-editable as it moves to Under Review.

  •  Review and Approve (f.e. by QA Officer)
    • The EBR enters Under Review. The QA Officer evaluates completeness and compliance, verifies that deviations and data collections are properly addressed, and either approves or rejects the EBR with an electronic
    • If rejected (from this step or during the subsequent release step), the Supervisor can generate a new draft version based on the current state to address required changes and resubmit. If the EBR is deemed not required, the Supervisor can cancel it, typically based on QA comments.

  • Review and Release (f.e. by QA Manager)
    • The QA Manager performs the final verification and releases or rejects the EBR with an electronic signature.
    • Release locks the EBR as a read-only, immutable record and can optionally generate the EBR Summary Report pdf. Released records can then support downstream processes such as batch disposition.
    • With a released EBR in SAP DM, the opportunity presents to close the loop with potential future integration towards SAP Batch Release Hub (BRH)

Key capabilities

The Electronic Batch Record POD template in Manage PODs 2.0 provides a consistent workspace to finalize batch records. Key components include:

  • EBR Table: Discover and navigate EBRs with status and basic details, then drill down for deeper review.
  • EBR Filter Bar: Quickly narrow to the records you need by plant, status, material, batch, dates, and more.
  • EBR SFC Table: See all SFCs associated with the batch, their execution status, and related context.
  • EBR Document Table: Review the full set of supporting documents ( such as work instructions, product history reports, NC attachments) that complement.
  • EBR Document Upload: Add supporting documents while in Draft state to ensure a complete, contextual record.
  • EBR Activity Log: Get an auditable timeline of actions, comments, and electronic signatures across Complete, Approve, and Release.
  • EBR E-Signature: Capture required electronic signatures at key control points; all signatures are recorded in the Activity Log.
  • Authorizations defined through scopes in BTP.
  • In future, when above capabilities become available, potential integration with the Batch Release Hub becomes the next step to integrate EBR in the full SAP suite.

Getting started checklist

  • Replicate Master Recipes and Routings from SAP S/4HANA and finalize execution-relevant master data (Work Instructions, Data Collections, tolerances, CPP/CQA) into a Master Batch Record Document.
  • Configure the Order POD and define which NCs are EBR-relevant for review-by-exception during execution.
  • Deploy the Electronic Batch Record POD template and set up e-signature policies for Draft submission, Approval, and Release.
  • Establish roles and permissions for Supervisors, QA Officers, and QA Managers.
  • Integrate with the EBR Create API (supported by GR Posting) to automate EBR initiation as part of your batch-close workflow.

 

Closing thought

EBR in SAP Digital Manufacturing brings your execution data, quality rigor, and governance into a single, controlled document-based flow—helping you close batches in 1 digital record with speed and confidence while maintaining a complete, signature-backed audit trail.

entry.title: Time Sheet Period is read as Null
entry.content:
I am setting up an error for the time sheet. I am attaching it to the Employee Time Sheet object so that it will only display when the user Submits the time sheet. The customer would like the Time Sheet Period to display in the message. I have set up the tags in the Message Definition. In the Business Rule, I have set it to read the Time Sheet Period. When the message is triggered, though, it comes back as Null. 

 

I see the time sheet period displayed in the Employee Time Sheet Object.

I have run a rule trace, and it is returning Null as the value. 

I have checked permissions and it looks like I have full permission to the Employee Time Sheet object.

Any ideas why this is not working?

Thanks,

Cindy

entry.title: Re: Is It possible to change table data model dynamically...
entry.content:
Hi @Susanne_Helbig ,

I’m currently facing an issue in SAC when dynamically switching models for a table.

I am using the following approach:

  • The model ID is passed via URL parameter
  • The table model is then set at runtime using:
    Table_1.setModel(modelId);

The setup is based on a Live Data Connection.

Problem:
When the model is assigned via setModel(), the table does not apply any of the expected default settings:

  • No filters are applied
  • All measures are displayed (ignoring any predefined visibility)
  • The table shows essentially the full dataset

However, when I assign the same model directly in the story designer:

  • The table correctly reflects the expected initial state (filters, measures, etc.)

Additionally, when opening the same model in the Data Analyzer, default filters (e.g. from BW variables or backend definitions) are applied as expected.

My question:
Is there any way to ensure that when using setModel():

  • the initial query state
  • backend variables / default filters
  • or measure visibility

are automatically applied?

Or is this behavior (ignoring query/default settings when using setModel) intended by design?

Any insights or recommended approaches for handling dynamic model selection while preserving initial query settings would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA - New Parallelization for Financial Data Quality General Ledger
entry.content:
We’re excited to announce a significant improvement to the Financial Data Quality (FDQ) General Ledger data collection within SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA.

To support large systems with high volumes of general ledger data, we have enhanced the parallelization logic for the Financial Data Quality check to package and process data, thereby utilizing available system resources more effectively and resulting in faster data collection runtimes.

What Was the Challenge?

Previously, the general ledger data collection within the SAP Readiness Check data collection framework limited parallelization to the fiscal year, resulting in one fiscal year per job as the most granular level for processing the workload. This processing logic created two problems:

  1. Unused capacity: If you configured 50 parallel jobs but only had 15 fiscal years of data, the data collection process could only use 15 jobs, leaving most of your configured capacity idle.
  2. Uneven workload: Some fiscal years contain far more documents than others. A job processing a high-volume year (like the current year) would run for hours or days, while jobs for older years finished quickly. The data collection process was only as fast as the slowest job.

For large systems, this meant the data collection process could take days or even weeks to complete.

What Has Changed?

The enhanced parallelization now distributes the workload more intelligently across all your configured jobs. The data collector can now split large workloads not just by fiscal year, but also by company code and document number ranges.

This improvement means:

  • Full utilization of your configured parallel jobs, regardless of how many fiscal years exist.
  • Automatic splitting of large company codes across multiple jobs for faster processing.
  • The workload is more balanced, enabling jobs to finish roughly at the same time.

Simplified Configuration

We have also simplified the expert mode configuration in the RC_COLLECT_ANALYSIS_DATA report. More specifically, we have removed the General Ledger (“fiscal years per job”) parameter from the “Number of Parallel Entities to Be Analyzed per Financial Area” configuration section.

Simplified Configuration

Now, you simply specify the number of parallel jobs, and the system automatically determines the optimal way to distribute workload across those jobs.

How Does It Work?

The enhanced processing logic uses a three-phase approach:

Phase 1: Costing
The system first counts documents for every combination of company code and fiscal year.

Phase 2: Splitting
Large combinations split automatically, further using document number ranges. For example, if Company Code 1000 in Fiscal Year 2024 contains 50 million documents, it automatically splits into multiple jobs, each processing a specific range of document numbers.

Phase 3: Balancing
Finally, all combinations and splits are distributed across the jobs, evenly balancing the workload. The system ensures that each job receives a similar amount of work, so they all finish at roughly the same time.

Dynamic Optimization

The system automatically adjusts to your data volume:

  • Optimal job sizing: The system calculates the optimal workload per job based on your total data volume and the configured parallel jobs.
  • Smart splitting decisions: Only combinations large enough to benefit from splitting are divided, while smaller ones are grouped efficiently.
  • Proportional allocation: Fiscal years and company codes with more documents receive a proportional share of processing resources.

What You’ll See in the Job Log

While the data collection process runs, the job log details the distribution of the workload:

What You’ll See in the Job Log

The log displays:

  • The number of identified company code-fiscal year combinations (the costing phase).
  • The splitting of large combinations, including the number of ranges (the splitting phase).
  • The distribution of combinations and splits across jobs (the balancing phase).
  • The details of each job’s assigned workload, including document number ranges where applicable.

How to Get Started

To enable the enhanced parallelization logic, implement the latest version of SAP Note 2972792 - Financial Data Quality: Trigger Data Collection Reports to Check Your Financial Data Quality and to Enable the Financial Data Quality Check in SAP Readiness Check.

As always, we look forward to receiving your feedback.

Your SAP Readiness Check Team

entry.title: [Webinar] Sessão de Boas-Vindas - SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Participe da Sessão de Boas-Vindas do SAP Cloud ALM e descubra como essa solução pode transformar a forma como você gerencia o ciclo de vida das suas aplicações SAP em ambientes cloud.

Registre-se aqui: https://events.teams.microsoft.com/event/16ad74a5-752e-43e1-aef2-919c40663768@42f7676c-f455-423c-82f6-dc2d99791af7

Neste webinar de abertura, você será apresentado à proposta do SAP Cloud ALM, entendendo como a plataforma apoia desde a implementação até a operação e otimização contínua dos seus sistemas. A sessão foi pensada para oferecer uma visão clara, objetiva e prática, preparando você para aproveitar ao máximo os próximos encontros e conteúdos da jornada.

 

Ao longo do evento, nossos especialistas irão contextualizar o uso do SAP Cloud ALM no cenário atual, destacar seus principais benefícios e mostrar como a solução contribui para maior transparência, eficiência operacional e governança dos projetos SAP.

Uma excelente oportunidade para iniciar sua jornada com o SAP Cloud ALM, alinhar expectativas e entender como essa ferramenta pode agregar valor real ao seu negócio.

 

Pré-requisitos

Solicitamos que você complete as seguintes etapas antes de participar do evento:

  1. Realize o processo de ativação do seu tenant. Acesse o link para ativar seu SAP Cloud ALM (caso ainda não tenha sido provisionado): https://me.sap.com/systemsprovisioning/provisioning.
  2. Como Solicitar SAP Cloud ALM no SAP for Me - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=axFjM01ZnU0
  3. Lembre-se do ID de usuário e da senha que foram usados para reivindicar o tenant do SAP Cloud ALM e também para o cockpit do BTP.
  4. Link para acessar o cockpit do BTP - https://account.hana.ondemand.com/#/home/welcome
  5. Verifique o e-mail de notification@sap.com para o link de acesso ao seu SAP Cloud ALM e os próximos passos.
  6. Link para acessar Configuração e Administração para SAP Cloud ALM - Configuração  https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/setup-administration/enabling-api.

entry.title: Integration between the S/4HANA on-prem and CDP through CPI (invoice & outbound delivery replicate)
entry.content:
Hello,

I am currently trying to configure the following APIs:

  • Customer Invoice – Send (B2B)
    Technical Name: CO_EDI_INVOICE_REQUEST_OUT

  • Outbound Delivery – Notify (B2B)
    Technical Name: DELIVERYREQUEST_OUT

The issue I am facing is as follows:

I accessed SOAMANAGER, searched using the technical names, and created manual configurations for the corresponding consumer proxies. In these configurations, I maintained the endpoints of the standard iFlows and the proper credentials:

  • Replicate Invoice from SAP S/4HANA to SAP Customer Data Platform

  • Replicate Outbound Delivery from SAP S/4HANA to SAP Customer Data Platform

These iFlows are part of the standard integration package “SAP S/4HANA Integration with SAP Customer Data Platform”, and the required credentials have been maintained correctly.

However, when creating an invoice and outbound delivery based on a sales order in SAP S/4HANA, only the sales order is successfully replicated to SAP Customer Data Platform via CPI. The invoice and outbound delivery are neither replicated nor visible as outbound messages in transaction SRT_MONI.

I would appreciate any guidance on what might be missing or misconfigured in this setup.

Best regards,
Ismail Waked

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Group Reporting: Using Automatic Postings for Revenue-Based Cost Distribution
entry.content:
Introduction

In Group Reporting, the financial "Close" process is frequently delayed by the reliance on external distribution cycles. Distributing shared costs or risks across entities often requires moving data between modules, creating silos, and slowing down the path to a consolidated view.

By utilizing Automatic Postings, organizations can implement simple logic directly within the consolidation engine. This native approach offers a transparent, audit-ready method for managing financial adjustments. This post shows how to automate the distribution of Bad Debt Allowance to profit centers based on Revenue drivers, ensuring your consolidated data reflects real-world credit exposure with clinical precision.

The Scenario: Revenue-Based Distribution

In this scenario, our objective is to distribute Bad Debt Allowance (FS Item 583000) across Profit Centers. The distribution is proportional to the Revenue (FS Item 411100 – Sale of Goods) generated by each profit center.

The underlying financial logic is straightforward: Higher Revenue → Higher Credit Exposure → Higher Bad Debt Allowance.

By automating this with the rules for automatic postings, we ensure that as revenue figures shift, the corresponding risk provision is redistributed without manual intervention or data.

Source Data (Before Distribution)

Results (After Distribution)

Solution Overview: The Modular Rule Design

To achieve this distribution, we employ a modular two-rule design. This structure allows the system first to establish the driver and then apply it to the financial values.

Rule ID

Purpose

Outcome

Rule 1

Calculate Revenue Share (%)

Calculates the percentage of total revenue per Profit Center and stores it as a quantity driver (%).

Rule 2

Distribute Bad Debt

Consumes the stored quantity driver (%) to post the proportional bad debt distribution.

Step 1: Calculating the Revenue Share (The Driver)

Rule 1 identifies the revenue for each Profit Center and determines its share relative to the total.

First, we define a selection, Z_SOG, to filter relevant source data. The criteria include:

  • FS Item: 411100 (Sale of Goods)
  • Posting levels: #, 0C, 00, 01
  • Profit center: not empty

Selection used in Rule 1:

Posting Logic & Variables To ensure the calculation is dynamic, we use the variable Z_VAR_SOG to represent the total revenue across all relevant Profit Centers. The system then calculates the share for each specific Profit Center using the following formula:

Revenue Share per profit center = SOURCE_LC / Z_VAR_SOG

Variable used in Rule 1:

Since the driver calculation must be processed within each consolidation unit, it is important to include the field Consolidation Unit in the list of matching fields of the variable.

Rule 1 Setting:

Record the result of this calculation to a statistical FS item, such as ZS_PC_SHR, and set the Base Unit to PRC (Percentage). This means that the FS item must be assigned to a breakdown category with unit of measure. Also, it is critical to enable "Posting with Quantities." Setting the unit to PRC ensures that a result of "0.15" is correctly interpreted as 15% during the multiplication in Step 2.

Step 2: Performing the Bad Debt Distribution

Once Rule 1 has prepared the drivers and stored them as quantities, Rule 2 executes the actual distribution. Rule 2 requires a Variable Z_VAR_BDA to select the total Bad Debt Allowance Amount.

Variable used in Rule 2:

Here again, it is important to include the field Consolidation Unit in the list of matching fields of the variable because the distribution must be processed within each consolidation unit.

The system applies the following formula to distribute the bad debt value across the target Profit Centers:

Distributed Value = Bad Debt * Z_PC_SHARE (Profit Center Share)

Selection used in Rule 2:

Rule 2 Setting:

The first posting line records the distributed amount on each profit center, and the second posting line reverses the full amount initially reported without profit center. Using the formula Balance() ensure that not rounding entry will remain.

 

Rule Group Setting:

The Rule Group includes above two rules, which are designed to work together in sequence.

The first rule prepares the driver, which is calculated based on the sales revenue for each profit center. This step is essential because it creates the weighting factor that will be used in the next step.

The second rule then uses this driver to distribute the bad debt across the profit centers. Since this distribution relies directly on the values calculated in the first rule, there is a clear dependency between the two.

To ensure everything runs correctly, the dependency is explicitly defined in the rule group by setting the second rule to “Depends on Previous Rules” . This guarantees that the first rule is executed before the second one. The sequence number further reflects this order, making sure the driver is prepared first, so the bad debt distribution can be calculated accurately based on it.

The result is a proportional distribution that accurately aligns risk provisions with revenue performance, visible in the task log .

Key Design Decisions & Why They Matter

  • Quantities as Drivers: By storing percentages as quantities, once a driver is calculated for a period, it remains in the database as a quantity that can be referenced by multiple rules without re-calculation.
  • Variable Usage (Z_VAR_SOG): Utilizing variables for the "Total" denominator ensures the calculation remains dynamic. If a new Profit Center is added or revenue data is updated, the variable automatically reflects the new total, ensuring the sum of shares always equals 100%.
  • Auditability via Document Types: We recommend dedicated Document Types (e.g., Z7, Z8) for these distributions. This isolates the distribution logic from original reported data, providing a clear trail for auditors to distinguish between original balances and calculated results.

Conclusion & Community Engagement

Working with the “Manage Rules for Automatic Postings” app is less technical than it may initially appear. In practice, defining simple distribution rules is quick and requires minimal effort—especially compared to other solutions that rely on scripting or complex calculation logic. A key advantage is how easily these basic setups can be extended to support more dynamic distribution scenarios, without introducing additional complexity. From a group reporting consultant’s perspective, this simplicity streamlines implementation and keeps teams flexible, while ensuring full audit transparency.

 

References:

SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting - Introducing New Rules for Automatic Postings 

SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting - Using Variables and Functions in Rules for Automatic Postings   

Rules for Automatic Postings 

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Special thanks to @GregoireDesombrefor his thoughtful review of this blog—much appreciated!

entry.title: SmartRecruiters Implementation & Product questions April 2026
entry.content:
Below are several SR questions with answers

 

Q1: Controlling Fields Visible to the Job Creator ("Originator")

SmartRecruiters does not have a dedicated "Originator" role in the traditional sense. However, you can control what the job creator sees through these mechanisms:

User Role at Creation Time

  • The person creating the job is governed by their assigned User Role (e.g., Recruiter, Hiring Manager, HR Admin). Field visibility and editability are tied to that role's configuration.
  • Go to Admin → User Roles → [Role] → Job Fields Permissions to configure which fields are visible, required, or editable per role.

"Create Job" Screen Configuration

  • SmartRecruiters allows you to configure the job creation wizard (the initial form) separately from the full job req form. You can limit which fields appear during the creation step via Admin → Company Settings → Jobs → Job Fields.
  • Fields can be set to: Hidden, Visible (read-only), or Editable per role.

Practical Workaround

  • Assign the job creator a specific role (e.g., "Job Creator" or "Recruiter") before they create the job. Since roles are user-level, not job-level at creation time, the user's default role governs what they see.
  • If you want stricter control, use Approval Workflows to lock fields after creation so post-creation editing is role-gated.

Key limitation: There is no dynamic "originator" role that auto-assigns based on who clicked "Create Job." The workaround is ensuring users have the correct default role pre-assigned.

Q2: Removing Standard Fields Like "Salary Range" from the Job Req

Yes, with limitations:

  • Custom job fields you create can be fully hidden or removed per role.
  • Standard/system fields (like Salary Range in the Position Information section) can often be hidden at the role level via Job Field Permissions, but cannot be permanently deleted from the system schema.
  • Navigate to: Admin → User Roles → [Role] → Job Fields and set Salary Range to "Hidden" for roles that should not see it.
  • For fields that are locked by SAP and cannot be hidden via UI, you would need to raise a support request with SmartRecruiters or use configuration consulting to suppress them.

Note: Some fields tied to integrations (e.g., HRIS sync) may be forcibly visible. Always test in a sandbox first.

 Q3: Preventing Candidates from Editing Application Answers After Submission

Out-of-the-box behavior: SmartRecruiters allows candidates to edit their application via the Candidate Portal until a certain stage in some configurations, but there is no native toggle to hard-lock answers immediately after submission.

Options available:

  • Application Form Design: Set application questions as non-editable by design — but this is limited natively.
  • Candidate Portal Settings: Under Admin → Career Site → Candidate Experience, review whether "Edit Application" is surfaced.
  • Workaround via Workflow Stage: Move candidates automatically to a stage (e.g., "Under Review") immediately upon submission using automation rules — some stage progressions restrict candidate-side edits.
  • Recommended: Raise this as a product enhancement request.

 

Q4: Candidate Profile & Logging Back In Without Applying

SmartRecruiters does have a Candidate Profile concept, though it works differently from some ATS platforms:

  • Candidates create a profile when they first apply via the Career Portal.
  • They can log back in at [yourcompany].smartrecruiters.com/candidate using the credentials created during application.
  • From their profile, they can: view application status, update profile details, and apply to new jobs — without needing to re-apply to access their account.
  • The profile stores resume, personal info, and application history.

To log in without applying:

  • Direct candidates to your Career Site → "Sign In" link. They can access their existing profile without initiating a new application.

If candidates report not being able to find the login, ensure the "Sign In" option is enabled and visible on your Career Site configuration.

 

Q5: Adding Job Field Tokens to Job Postings / Advertisements

This is a known limitation in SmartRecruiters' Career Page builder:

Standard Behavior:

  • The Job Ad / Posting template supports a limited set of merge tokens (job title, department, location, etc.).
  • The Career Page drag-and-drop editor natively exposes only ~2 job-level tokens for dynamic content.

Workarounds to bring in custom job fields as tokens:

Approach

Details

Job Description Body

Embed custom field values directly in the job description text manually or via an integration

API Integration

Use the SmartRecruiters API to pull job fields and inject them into a custom-built career page outside the native builder

Custom Career Site (CSS/JS)

If you use a custom-hosted career site, you can call the SR Open API to fetch and render any job field dynamically

SmartRecruiters Professional Services

Request custom token support — enterprise agreements sometimes allow extended token sets

Best practice: For rich, field-driven postings, consider building a custom career site using SR's Open API rather than relying on the native page builder.

 

Q6: Best Practices for Creating a Non-Position-Managed Evergreen Req

An Evergreen Req is used for continuous/high-volume hiring where you pool candidates over time.

Setup Best Practices:

  1. Create as a "Pipeline" or "Pooling" Job
    • Do not tie it to a specific headcount or position in your org chart.
    • Set Number of Openings to a high number (e.g., 99) or use "Unlimited" if available.
  2. Naming Convention
    • Use a clear naming pattern: "[Department] – Evergreen Pool – [Year]" to distinguish from active reqs.
  3. Job Status Management
    • Keep the job in "Always Open" status; use Automation Rules to move candidates from the evergreen req to specific active reqs when a real opening arises.
  4. Sourcing
    • Post to job boards with evergreen-appropriate messaging ("Join our talent community").
    • Use Talent Pool tagging to segment candidates within the req.
  5. Avoid Offer/Hire Actions Directly on the Evergreen Req
    • Use it purely as a sourcing and screening pipeline; transfer finalists to a positional req for offer and hire steps to keep analytics clean.
  6. Review Cadence
    • Set a periodic review (monthly/quarterly) to disposition stale candidates to maintain pool quality.

 

Q7: What Drives Hiring Cost Calculations in the Analytics Dashboard?

SmartRecruiters' Cost of Hire analytics pulls from:

Cost Driver

Source

Job Board Posting Costs

Integrated via SmartDistribute (vonq) or manually entered job board budgets

Agency/Vendor Fees

Entered via the Offer or Vendor Management module

Internal Recruiter Cost

Configured as a daily/hourly rate per recruiter in Admin settings

Time-to-Fill × Recruiter Rate

Calculated automatically based on req open date and recruiter cost rate

Referral Bonuses

If tracked within SR

To ensure accurate data:

  • Set up Recruiter Cost Rates under Admin → Analytics Settings.
  • Ensure job board spend is fed back via SmartDistribute integration (vonq)
  • Configure your cost center mappings if using HRIS integration.

 

Q8: Posting a Job to Multiple Locations in the Same Country

Since Location is a single-select field, options include:

  • Duplicate the Req — Create one req per location; link them via a common requisition family or job family code for reporting.
  • Use a Generic Location — Set location to the country or region level (e.g., "Germany – Multiple Locations") and specify details in the job description body.
  • Multi-Location Field (if enabled) — Some SR configurations support a secondary "Additional Locations" field; check with your SR admin if this is active on your instance.
  • SmartRecruiters Multi-Location Feature — Enterprise plans may have access to a multi-location posting feature — confirm with your CSM.

 

Q9: Posting a Job in Multiple Languages

SmartRecruiters handles multilingual postings as follows:

Native Approach:

  • Each language version of a job requires a separate job req or a separate job ad within the same req.
  • Under the job, go to Job Ad → Add Language Version — this allows you to create translated versions of the posting text within a single req.

Steps:

  1. Open the Job → Job Ad tab
  2. Click "Add Language"
  3. Select the target language and enter the translated content
  4. Publish — candidates will see the version matching their browser/portal language setting

Note: Job fields (title, department, etc.) remain in the base language. Only the Job Ad body (description, requirements) supports multi-language versions natively.

 

Q10: Blocking Candidates from Changing Interview Timeslots After Self-Scheduling

Native SmartRecruiters behavior:

  • Once a candidate self-schedules via SmartRecruiters Interview Scheduling, they can typically reschedule until the interview time if the link/portal remains active.

Options to restrict rescheduling:

  • Expire the Scheduling Link — After the candidate books, manually expire or deactivate the scheduling link so it can no longer be used to change the slot.
  • Automation Rule — Trigger an automation once a slot is booked to move the candidate to a stage where the scheduling action is no longer available.
  • Communicate via Confirmation Email — Use the confirmation email template to set the expectation that rescheduling requires contacting the recruiter directly (soft control).
  • Third-Party Scheduling Integration — If using GoodTime, Calendly, or Prelude via integration, those tools offer hard locks on rescheduling after a configurable window.

Hard technical lock on self-scheduling changes is not natively available in SR out of the box. The most robust solution is using a dedicated scheduling integration that supports rescheduling restrictions.

entry.title: job level - pay range association
entry.content:
Hi ,

We want the pay range to be determined solely based on job level, which is maintained in a custom MDF object within Job Classification, rather than the pay grade maintained in Job Information. 

Specifically, the pay range should be derived from a custom MDF field (ABC) in Job Information, then Job Level(12) in the Job Classification object. We do not want the Pay Grade (SG1) foundation object to be part of this association. However, all employees will continue to have a pay grade assigned in Job Information, and we do not intend to change that.

Given this scenario, please advise on the best approach to create the pay range association in the Corporate Data Model. We also want to use these pay ranges in Compensation Planning, 

 

Thank you

entry.title: 🚀 CodeJam Curitiba: quando a comunidade mostra a sua força
entry.content:
Tem eventos que a gente organiza...E tem eventos que a gente vive...O CodeJam Curitiba foi vivido.

Desde cedo já dava para sentir que seria especial. A recepção com coffee, conversas animadas e aquela energia boa de quem estava ali para aprender de verdade. E, claro, a presença das lendas Thomas, Rich e Josh já elevava o nível da expectativa.

 

 

 

 

Às 9h iniciamos oficialmente. Nosso curador  @LuizSouza abriu o evento reforçando algo que eu acredito profundamente: comunidade não é sobre evento, é sobre conexão. É sobre troca real.

 

 

 

 

Na sequência, o professor Valter, representando a Escola Politécnica PUCPR, falou sobre a importância dessa aproximação entre universidade e mercado. E que privilégio poder realizar esse momento dentro de uma instituição que abriu as portas com tanto carinho.

 

 

 

 

Nosso patrocinador CHPRO, por meio do Marcelo Passaglia, trouxe algo que eu particularmente valorizo muito: trajetória. Histórias inspiram. Pessoas reais inspiram. E ver esse apoio genuíno ao ecossistema mostra que estamos no caminho certo.

 

 

 

 

E então, Josh. Energia pura. Daquelas que contagiam, que lembram por que a SAP Community é diferente.

 

 

 

 

 

🤖Mão na massa — e é aqui que a mágica acontece

Depois das falas, fomos para o hands-on. E que experiência.

Thomas e Rich trouxeram muitas novidades sobre IA e mostraram, na prática, o tamanho das possibilidades que temos nas mãos. Mas o que mais me marcou não foi só o conteúdo técnico.

 

 

 

Foi a proximidade.

Ver Thomas e Rich lado a lado com os participantes, sentados juntos, trocando, ensinando, aprendendo… isso é comunidade de verdade. Não é palco distante. É construção conjunta.

Na parte da tarde, o ambiente era de concentração, curiosidade e descoberta. Era bonito de ver.

🎁Encerramento com aquele sentimento de “quero mais”

Finalizamos com o tradicional sorteio, a foto oficial e mais um coffee para aquele networking que gera futuras conexões e oportunidades.

 

 

 

 

 

E sim… nossa mascote capivara marcou presença e roubou sorrisos por onde passou 🧡

Encerramos o dia com aquele sentimento coletivo de que algo importante começou.

💙Gratidão (daquelas sinceras)

Eu acredito que eventos assim só acontecem porque existem pessoas que acreditam junto.

Nossa gratidão:

À @antonia_zorn , da SAP Community, por confiar, escolher Curitiba e nos incluir na rota.

Ao Thomas e Rich, por compartilharem conhecimento com tanta generosidade.

Ao Josh, pela energia e por fortalecer continuamente essa comunidade global.

Ao nosso patrocinador CHPRO, que apoiou de coração aberto e tornou tudo possível.

À Escola Politécnica PUCPR, por nos receber como casa.

À Vitória e ao Vitor, que vieram da SAP São Leopoldo para apoiar os instrutores e fortalecer ainda mais a experiência do CodeJam.

Aos nossos curadores @SimoneAzevedo , @LuizSouza e @AdrianoCWB , que planejaram e executaram cada detalhe com dedicação.

 

 

 

 

 

E um agradecimento muito especial aos participantes.

Vocês se inscreveram, confiaram, compareceram e provaram algo muito importante:

Curitiba tem sede de conhecimento. Curitiba tem protagonismo. Curitiba tem força.

Vocês mostraram, na prática, o poder da nossa comunidade quando se une em torno de tecnologia, aprendizado e colaboração.

 

🎬Veja os melhores momentos

Os registros estão no vídeo Melhores momentos— mas a experiência, essa fica na memória.

Curitiba entrou no mapa do CodeJam.

E eu tenho certeza: esse é só o começo.

Que venham muitos outros.

Que venham mais conexões.

Que venham mais histórias para contar.

Nos vemos nos próximos eventos da SAP Community Curitiba.

Com carinho,

Simone Azevedo 💙

entry.title: RAG For Document Analysis
entry.content:
Introduction

Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed how we interact with data, enabling natural language understanding and generation at unprecedented levels. Yet, despite their capabilities, they are inherently limited by the knowledge embedded during training and often referred to as parametric memory. This limitation becomes evident when models are tasked with answering questions that require up-to-date, proprietary, or domain-specific information.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) emerges as a powerful paradigm to address this gap. By integrating external knowledge sources into the generation process, RAG enables LLMs to produce responses that are not only coherent but also factually grounded and contextually relevant.

Understanding the RAG Paradigm

At its core, RAG separates knowledge storage from reasoning capability.

  • Parametric Knowledge: Encoded within the model during training
  • Non-Parametric Knowledge: Stored externally in a searchable repository

This separation allows systems to remain flexible and continuously updated without the need for expensive retraining.

A useful analogy is the distinction between a closed-book exam and an open-book exam. Traditional LLMs operate like the former, relying solely on what they have learned. RAG-enabled systems, however, can reference external materials, enabling more accurate and informed responses.

The RAG Workflow

 

 

A typical RAG pipeline consists of these stages: 

  • Document Ingestion and Chunking

The process begins with ingesting documents from various sources such as PDFs, Word files, or structured data formats. These documents are divided into smaller, manageable segments or chunks. Effective chunking is critical, as it directly impacts the quality of retrieval.

  • Embedding Generation

Each chunk is transformed into a vector representation using an embedding model. These embeddings capture the semantic meaning of the text, allowing similar pieces of information to be located efficiently.

  • Vector Storage

The embeddings are stored in a vector database, such as Chroma, FAISS, or Pinecone. This database functions as an external memory layer, enabling rapid similarity searches.

  • Retrieval

When a user submits a query, it is converted into an embedding and compared against the stored vectors. The system retrieves the top-k most relevant chunks, forming the contextual foundation for the response.

  • Augmentation

The retrieved content is combined with the user query using a structured prompt template. This step ensures the language model has access to the most relevant information before generating a response.

  • Generation

Finally, the augmented prompt is passed to the LLM, which synthesizes a response grounded in both its internal knowledge and the retrieved context.

Why RAG Over Fine-Tuning?

Historically, adapting models to domain-specific tasks required fine-tuning, a process that is resource-intensive and inflexible. RAG offers a more efficient alternative:

  • Dynamic Knowledge Updates: No need to retrain the model when data changes
  • Cost Efficiency: Eliminates repeated training cycles
  • Scalability: Easily adapts to growing datasets
  • Modularity: Components can be independently optimized

This makes RAG particularly well-suited for environments where information evolves rapidly.

Benefits of RAG

Organizations adopting RAG can expect several advantages:

  • Improved Accuracy: Responses are grounded in real, retrievable data
  • Reduced Hallucinations: Minimizes unsupported or fabricated outputs
  • Real-Time Relevance: Easily incorporates the latest information
  • Transparency: Enables traceability to source documents
  • Domain Adaptability: Works seamlessly with specialized datasets

Challenges and Considerations

Despite its advantages, implementing RAG effectively requires careful design:

  • Chunking Strategy: Poor segmentation can degrade retrieval quality
  • Embedding Selection: Model choice significantly impacts performance
  • Latency: Retrieval adds overhead to response time
  • Prompt Engineering: Poorly structured prompts can limit effectiveness
  • Evaluation Metrics: Measuring success requires more than traditional accuracy metrics

Addressing these challenges is essential for building robust, production-ready systems.

Conclusion

Retrieval-Augmented Generation represents a significant evolution in the design of intelligent systems. By decoupling knowledge from reasoning, it enables LLMs to operate with greater accuracy, flexibility, and relevance.

For document analysis, RAG is not just an enhancement, it is a foundational capability that transforms how organizations interact with information. As data continues to grow in volume and complexity, RAG will play a central role in enabling systems that are not only intelligent, but also reliable and context-aware.

entry.title: Reforma Tributária Brasil 2026: Impactos no SAP e o "Plano de Voo" para a Transição (Convite)
entry.content:
Olá, SAP Community!

Já estamos em 2026 e a "fase de teoria" da Reforma Tributária brasileira acabou. Para nós, profissionais que trabalhamos com o ecossistema SAP, este é o momento em que a legislação sai do papel e precisa rodar dentro do sistema.

O grande desafio técnico e de negócios deste ano não é apenas a nova alíquota, mas sim a convivência dos sistemas tributários. Como manter o seu ERP (seja ECC ou S/4HANA) operando com o modelo atual enquanto inicia os testes e validações do IVA Dual (IBS + CBS)?

A complexidade de localização no Brasil sempre foi alta, e agora, com a necessidade de um "motor de cálculo" híbrido durante a transição, o risco de paradas operacionais ou inconsistências fiscais aumenta significativamente.

O Plano de Voo para 2026

Para discutir exatamente como superar esses desafios técnicos e estratégicos, a CVA, parceira especializada em consultoria SAP, uniu forças com a Avalara, líder em automação fiscal.

Na próxima semana, dia 21 de Janeiro (quarta-feira), realizaremos um webinar focado em casos práticos e arquitetura de solução.

Não vamos falar (apenas) de leis. Vamos falar de como a tecnologia suporta a lei.

O que vamos abordar:

  • Cenário Q1 2026: O que priorizar na configuração do sistema agora no início do ano.

  • Desafio da Convivência: Como gerenciar a apuração antiga e a nova simultaneamente sem customizações excessivas (Z) que engessam o core digital.

  • Automação via Tax Engine: Como a integração nativa da Avalara resolve a complexidade de cálculo, tirando o peso do ERP.

  • Cases: Lições aprendidas de projetos que já iniciaram a adaptação.

Os Palestrantes:

Teremos uma discussão técnica e executiva com:

  • Juliano Kisielewicz (Chief Revenue Officer na CVA) – Trazendo a visão de processos e estratégia SAP.

  • Luiz Fernando Albertin (Sales Engineer Head na Avalara) – Trazendo a visão técnica da solução fiscal.

Como Participar:

O evento é online e gratuito, aberto a consultores, arquitetos de solução, key-users e gestores fiscais que utilizam SAP.

📅 Data: 21 de Janeiro de 2026
🕙 Horário: 10h00
🔗Link para inscriçao

Se você está trabalhando na estratégia de localização da sua empresa para este ano, este é um conteúdo essencial para o seu planejamento.

Nos vemos lá!

entry.title: AP Aging Query Not Matching SAP Business One Aging Report
entry.content:
Hi Experts,
I am currently developing a custom query for the Accounts Payable Aging report in SAP Business One. However, the total amount returned by my query does not match the standard SAP Business One Aging report.
Below is the query I am using:

SELECT T0.[DocNum], T0.[CardCode], T0.[CardName], T0.[DocDate], T0.[DocTotal] FROM OPCH T0 INNER JOIN OVPM T1 ON T0.[ReceiptNum] = T1.[DocEntry] INNER JOIN OACT T2 ON T0.[CtlAccount] = T2.[AcctCode] WHERE
T0.[CANCELED] ='N' and T0.[DocDate] <=[%0]
and
(T0.[DocStatus]='O' or T0.[DocStatus]<>'O' and T1.[DocDate] >[%0])
and
(
T2.[AcctCode]='403100' or
T2.[AcctCode]='403200' or
T2.[AcctCode]='403300' or
T2.[AcctCode]='403400' or
T2.[AcctCode]='403500'
) and T1.[Canceled] <>'Y'

My requirement is to include:
Open invoices as of the selected date
Closed invoices that were paid after the selected date
Anyone can help me please

entry.title: SAP DM Inventory Transfer Valuation TYPE
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

I am facing an issue while performing a 311 movement for a non-batch-managed PRT material that is subject to split valuation.

During the MIGO transfer posting in S/4HANA, the system prompts for a valuation type in order to post the document. However, when the corresponding IDoc is generated for inventory download, the system incorrectly populates the batch number field with the valuation type.

This results in two issues:

  1. Goods movement failure (311 transfer posting):
    The system attempts to fetch a batch based on the batch number, which in this case is actually the valuation type. Since the material is not batch-managed, the posting fails.
  2. Goods issue restriction in consumption plugin:
    When attempting goods issuance, the system requires a valuation type to be entered. However, the material consumption plugin does not provide a field to input or select a valuation type during consumption, making it impossible to proceed with the goods issue.

Has anyone encountered a similar behavior in S/4HANA or SAP DM integration scenarios involving split valuation materials and non-batch-managed PRTs? Any guidance or recommended configuration would be greatly appreciated.

Best regards,
Aziz Khan

SAP Digital Manufacturing SAP Digital Manufacturing Insights

entry.title: Subject: Clarification on Address Data Passed in Vertex Integration for SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi all,

We are currently evaluating the standard integration between SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and Vertex for tax determination and had a question around how address data is handled in the delivered integration.

From the SAP-delivered Vertex integration flows (Calculate Tax, Post Tax, and Determine Tax Jurisdiction Code), we understand that the integration is standardized and relies on predefined APIs/iFlows. However, it is not entirely clear from the available documentation what level of address detail is included in the request sent to Vertex.

Architecture context:
Our current landscape includes:

  • Front-end order entry systems (OES / eCommerce platforms)

  • SAP BTP Integration Suite as the middleware layer

  • SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud as the system of record

  • Vertex as the external tax engine

We are evaluating two possible patterns:

  1. SAP-driven tax calculation:
    Front-end → SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud → Vertex

  2. Upstream tax calculation via integration layer:
    Front-end → Integration Suite → Vertex → SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

Questions:

  • What address elements are passed from SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to Vertex as part of tax calculation or jurisdiction determination?

  • Is street-level address (e.g., street/house number) included in the payload, or is the integration primarily based on higher-level location data (e.g., ZIP/postal code, region)?

  • Do customers have visibility into or control over the payload being sent to Vertex in the standard Public Cloud integration?

  • For U.S. tax scenarios where jurisdiction accuracy can depend on street-level detail, how is this handled in the standard integration approach?

We are trying to determine whether the standard SAP-driven integration is sufficient for these requirements, or if tax determination is typically handled upstream (e.g., via middleware) to allow for more control and accuracy.

Any guidance or insights from those who have implemented Vertex with SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: SAP Public cloud analytics app and report
entry.content:
Dear @arturo_suarez,

List of the main standard apps and analytics related capabilities relevant for Manufacturing and Advanced Variant Configuration in Public Cloud

Manufacturing:

  • Manage Work Center Capacity (F3289)
  • Capacity Scheduling Table (F3770)
  • Analyze Production Costs Event Based (F4059)
  • Order Cost Details Event Based (F4254)
  • Event Based Solution Monitor Product Costing (F5133)
  • Manage Event Based Posting Errors Product Costing (F5132)
  • Postprocess Event Based Postings Overhead Calculation (F5763)

  • Record Results for Inspection Points (F2689)
  • Production Cost Overview Event Based (F6248)

  • Manage Process Order Operations (F5323)

Advanced Variant Configuration:

  • VC Modeling Environment (PMEVC)
  • Simulate Configuration Models (F2570)

  • Order Engineering Worklist (F4770)
  • Variant Matrix Reuse Library Master Data (F3042)
  • Variant configuration Overview(F2940)
  • Custom CDS Views Version 2 (F1866A)
  • Custom Analytical Queries and Services (F1572A)
  • Query Browser (F1068)
  • View Browser (F2170)
  • Manage KPIs and Reports (F2814)
  • Analytical Queries (F1572)

More details below:

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/homePage

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Handling Dynamic Fields in SAP RAP using JSON Payload in CDS Custom Entity
entry.content:
Introduction

Core Data Services (CDS) entities in the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) are usually constructed using statically set parameters. Although this guarantees robust typing and clarity, it becomes restrictive when programs have to deal with dynamic inputs like changing fields, tables, or runtime-driven filters.

Such adaptability is crucial in many real-world situations. Nevertheless, dynamic parameter structures are not supported by CDS natively, which frequently results in complicated and difficult-to-maintain designs.

This blog describes an adaptable method that passes dynamic field data as a single parameter and processes it at the ABAP layer using a JSON payload inside a CDS Custom Entity. This keeps the CDS design straightforward while enabling more flexible and scalable RAP solutions.

What are CDS Custom Entities in RAP?

A CDS Custom Entity is a unique kind of CDS entity that does not read data directly from the database in the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP). Rather, an ABAP class is used to accomplish it, and the developer has complete control over the data retrieval mechanism.

In contrast to Standard CDS Views:

  • Custom Entities enable ABAP's dynamic data processing.
  • When logic cannot be managed only at the database level, they are usually employed.
  • They offer the freedom to execute intricate or unconventional scenarios, including external integrations or dynamic queries.

Because the data is fetched and processed in the ABAP layer, Custom Entities are well-suited for scenarios where:

  • Input parameters need to be interpreted dynamically
  • Data sources or structures vary at runtime
  • Standard CDS capabilities are not sufficient

Real-Time Business Scenario: Dynamic Field Validation

In some SAP applications, there is a requirement to validate fields dynamically based on user input.

For instance, a user or an outside system might submit a request such as:

  • Verify if a table contains the specified fields.
  • During runtime, dynamically provide field values
  • Tables and fields are not predetermined.

One Sample input would be:

  • Table: SFLIGHT
  • Fields: FLDATE and CARRID
  • Values: LH, 20260313 

The table and fields can be entirely different in a different request. This means that any table and any field must be handled dynamically by the application without the need for preset parameters.

The Problem

If we attempt to use normal CDS to implement this:

  • Each field would require its own set of parameters.
  • As fields alter, the number of parameters would increase.
  • Unknown or dynamic fields cannot be handled.

The Restrictions:
Flexible structures like a "list of fields" cannot be dynamically accepted by CDS. Because of this, the design is inflexible and challenging to scale.

The Solution

We use a single argument (FieldValuesJson) to deliver all field details as a JSON payload in order to resolve this.

There are other entries in this JSON, such as:

  • Table name
  • field name
  • field value

In the ABAP Implementation:

  • An internal table is created by parsing the JSON.
  • Every field is dynamically verified using the Data Dictionary (DD03L).
  • The existence of the field is indicated by the result that is returned.

Without altering the CDS definition, this method enables the application to dynamically handle any table and any field.

Implementation Using CDS Custom Entity

Step 1: Create CDS Custom Entity

First, define a CDS Custom Entity to accept input parameter and return results.

@EndUserText.label: 'Dynamic Field Filter Custom Entity'
@ObjectModel.query.implementedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_DYN_FIELD_QUERY'

define root custom entity ZI_DYN_FIELD_ENTITY
{
  .selectionField: [{ position: 10 }]
  @EndUserText.label: 'Object Type'
  key ObjectType      : abap.char(30);

  .selectionField: [{ position: 20 }]
  @EndUserText.label: 'Policy Type'
  key PolicyType      : abap.char(20);
  
  @EndUserText.label: 'Table Name'
  key Tabname         : abap.char(30);

  @EndUserText.label: 'Field Name'
  key Fieldname       : abap.char(30);

  .selectionField: [{ position: 30 }]
  @EndUserText.label: 'Get Destructible'
  GetDestructible     : abap.char(1);

  .selectionField: [{ position: 40 }]
  @EndUserText.label: 'Field Values JSON'
  FieldValuesJson     : abap.char(1333);

  @EndUserText.label: 'Field Value'
  FieldValue          : abap.char(100);

  @EndUserText.label: 'Result Info'
  ResultInfo          : abap.char(200);
}

Explanation:

  • FieldValuesJson is used to pass dynamic field data as JSON
  • Output fields (Tabname, Fieldname, FieldValue, ResultInfo) hold processed results
  • The annotation @ObjectModel.query.implementedBy links to the ABAP class

Step 2: Create Behavior Definition (Unmanaged)

Define a behavior Definition for the custom entity:

unmanaged implementation in class zcl_dyn_field_query unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for ZI_DYN_FIELD_ENTITY
authorization master ( global )
lock master
{
  field ( readonly ) ObjectType;
  field ( readonly ) PolicyType;
  field ( readonly ) Tabname;
  field ( readonly ) Fieldname;
}

Explanation:

  • Unmanaged behavior is used since logic is fully handled in ABAP
  • Fields are marked as readonly because this is a read-only scenario
  • No CRUD operations are required

Step 3: Implement Query Provider Class

Create the ABAP Class and implement IF_RAP_QUERY_PROVIDER:

CLASS zcl_dyn_field_query DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
  " RAP Query Provider Interface for Custom Entity
    INTERFACES if_rap_query_provider.

  " Structure to hold dynamic field input from JSON
    TYPES:
      BEGIN OF ty_s_field_input,
        tabname   TYPE string,
        fieldname TYPE string,
        value     TYPE string,
      END OF ty_s_field_input,
      ty_t_field_input TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_s_field_input
                       WITH DEFAULT KEY.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
   " Result structure based on CDS Custom Entity
    TYPES ty_t_result TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF zi_dyn_field_entity
                      WITH DEFAULT KEY.

   " Method to parse JSON payload into internal table
    METHODS parse_field_values_json
      IMPORTING iv_json      TYPE string
      RETURNING VALUE(rt_fv) TYPE ty_t_field_input.

   " Method to apply validation logic on dynamic fields
    METHODS apply_rules
      IMPORTING it_field_values TYPE ty_t_field_input
                iv_object_type  TYPE string
                iv_policy_type  TYPE string
                iv_destructible TYPE string
      RETURNING VALUE(rt_result) TYPE ty_t_result.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_dyn_field_query IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_rap_query_provider~select.

   " Variables to hold filter inputs
    DATA lv_object_type  TYPE string.
    DATA lv_policy_type  TYPE string.
    DATA lv_destructible TYPE string.
    DATA lv_json         TYPE string.
    DATA lt_result       TYPE ty_t_result.

    " 1. Extract filters from the Odata request
    TRY.
        DATA(lo_filter)      = io_request->get_filter( ).
        DATA(lt_filter_cond) = lo_filter->get_as_ranges( ).

        LOOP AT lt_filter_cond INTO DATA(ls_filter).
          CASE ls_filter-name.
            WHEN 'OBJECTTYPE'.
              lv_object_type  = ls_filter-range[ 1 ]-low.
            WHEN 'POLICYTYPE'.
              lv_policy_type  = ls_filter-range[ 1 ]-low.
            WHEN 'GETDESTRUCTIBLE'.
              lv_destructible = ls_filter-range[ 1 ]-low.
            WHEN 'FIELDVALUESJSON'.
              lv_json         = ls_filter-range[ 1 ]-low.
          ENDCASE.
        ENDLOOP.
      CATCH cx_root.
        " Optional: Handle Invalid filter scenario
    ENDTRY.

    " 2. Convert JSON payload into internal table
    DATA(lt_fv) = parse_field_values_json( lv_json ).

    " 3. Execute dynamic validation logic
    lt_result = apply_rules(
      it_field_values = lt_fv
      iv_object_type  = lv_object_type
      iv_policy_type  = lv_policy_type
      iv_destructible = lv_destructible ).

    " 4. Handle case where no valid input is provided
    IF lt_result IS INITIAL.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        objecttype      = lv_object_type
        policytype      = lv_policy_type
        getdestructible = lv_destructible
        fieldvaluesjson = lv_json
        resultinfo      = 'No valid JSON fields provided'
      ) TO lt_result.
    ENDIF.

   " 5. Handle total record count (for paging support)
    DATA lv_total TYPE int8.
    lv_total = lines( lt_result ).

    IF io_request->is_total_numb_of_rec_requested( ).
      io_response->set_total_number_of_records( lv_total ).
    ENDIF.

   " 6. Apply paging and return response data
    IF io_request->is_data_requested( ).
      DATA(lo_paging) = io_request->get_paging( ).
      DATA(lv_offset) = lo_paging->get_offset( ).
      DATA(lv_limit)  = lo_paging->get_page_size( ).

      IF lv_limit = if_rap_query_paging=>page_size_unlimited.
        io_response->set_data( lt_result ).
      ELSE.
        DATA lt_paged LIKE lt_result.

        " Apply offset + limit slicing
        APPEND LINES OF lt_result FROM ( lv_offset + 1 ) TO ( lv_offset + lv_limit ) TO lt_paged.
        io_response->set_data( lt_paged ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD parse_field_values_json.
    " Return empty if no JSON is provided
    IF iv_json IS INITIAL.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.

    " Deserialize JSON into ABAP structure
    /ui2/cl_json=>deserialize(
      EXPORTING
        json = iv_json
      CHANGING
        data = rt_fv
    ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD apply_rules.

    " Loop through each dynamic field input
    LOOP AT it_field_values INTO DATA(ls_fv).
      DATA ls_result TYPE zi_dyn_field_entity.
      CLEAR ls_result.

      " Check if field exists in Data Dictionary
      SELECT SINGLE fieldname
        FROM dd03l
        WHERE tabname   = _fv-tabname
          AND fieldname = _fv-fieldname
        INTO (lv_fname).

      " Map input and context data to result structure
      ls_result-objecttype      = iv_object_type.
      ls_result-policytype      = iv_policy_type.
      ls_result-getdestructible = iv_destructible.
      ls_result-tabname         = ls_fv-tabname.
      ls_result-fieldname       = ls_fv-fieldname.
      ls_result-fieldvalue      = ls_fv-value.

      " Set validation result message
      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        ls_result-resultinfo = |Found: { ls_fv-tabname }-{ ls_fv-fieldname }|.
      ELSE.
        ls_result-resultinfo = |Error: { ls_fv-tabname }-{ ls_fv-fieldname } not in DDIC|.
      ENDIF.

      APPEND ls_result TO rt_result.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

Result:

Field inputs given as a JSON payload can be dynamically processed by the service once the CDS Custom Entity and query provider logic have been implemented.

When the following request is made to the service:

/sap/opu/odata4/.../ZI_XXXXX?
$filter=
ObjectType eq 'BC_SFLIGHT'
and PolicyType eq 'DEMO'
and GetDestructible eq 'X'
and FieldValuesJson eq
'[{"tabname":"SFLIGHT","fieldname":"CARRID","value":"LH"},{"tabname":"SFLIGHT","fieldname":"FLDATE","value":"20260313"}]'

Conclusion

In this blog, we explored the challenge of handling dynamic fields in RAP when working with statically defined CDS parameters. We discussed a real-time scenario where field names, tables, and values are not fixed and need to be processed dynamically.

To address this, we implemented a solution using a CDS Custom Entity and passed dynamic input as a JSON payload, which is parsed and processed in the ABAP query provider class. This allowed us to validate fields against the Data Dictionary without modifying the CDS structure.

Overall, this approach provides a flexible and reusable solution for dynamic field handling in RAP, making it easier to adapt to changing requirements while keeping the design simple and maintainable.

entry.title: Venue for SAP Inside Track 2026 Announced!
entry.content:
We’re excited to announce that SAP Inside Track Sydney 2026 will be held at The Mint on Friday, September 4.

As one of Sydney’s most historic and character-filled venues, The Mint brings a unique sense of place to this year’s event.  Its heritage architecture and rich history create a memorable backdrop, while the striking modern auditorium and beautiful surrounds offer the perfect setting for a fresh, elevated SAP Inside Track experience.

Its CBD location on Macquarie Street is highly convenient for attendees, within walking distance of Martin Place, Town Hall and Wynyard.

This combination of history, design, and central city convenience makes The Mint an ideal home for a reimagined SIT Sydney — one that brings the SAP community together in a setting for learning, conversation, connection and inspiration.

🗓️ Date: Friday, September 4, 2026 | 9am to 6pm
📍 Location: The Mint, 10 Macquarie Street, Sydney

More details coming soon, including registration, call for speakers, and sponsorship opportunities.

We can’t wait to welcome you at The Mint!

#SAPInsideTrack #SITSydney #SAPCommunity #SAPChampions

entry.title: SAP Joule: Conversational insights for production and process orders
entry.content:
SAP Joule introduced conversational capabilities directly into the Fiori Launchpad, and users can now interact with a SAP S/4HANA system using natural language. As of SAP S/4HANA 2021, Private Cloud Edition, Joule also supports manufacturing orders, and users can now use it to gain insights about production and process orders. The production scheduler can now use Joule to get, for example, a list of production orders for a material, show details of a specific order or get details about items or operations of an order.

This blog will show details about the features available for production orders.

Pre-requisites

First of all, Production and Process Order specific capabilities should be available as of SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud 2021.

In order to use the Joule capabilities described in this blog, you need to have Joule active in the system. You can find a detailed Joule setup guide in the following blog:

  • Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - A Comprehensive Setup Guide

The implementation of SAP Notes 3694923 and 3703307 is required to allow some actions to be completed in the respective Fiori App.

Users with access to Joule will need roles SAP_BR_PRODN_SUPERVISOR_DISC and SAP_BR_PRODN_SUPERVISOR_PROC to be able to use the production and process orders use cases.

Some use cases require the user to have an Area of Responsibility defined for the production supervisor. You can find more details about how to define an Area of Responsibility in the following link:

  • My Area of Responsibility – Production Supervisor.

Use cases

Joule supports the following uses cases for Production Orders:

  • Show all production orders
  • Show production order details
  • Show production orders for a plant and/or production supervisor
  • Show a specific detail of a production order
  • Show all items of a production order
  • Show all operations of a production order
  • Show master output material data
  • Find material details for a production order
  • Show statuses of a production order
  • Check status of a production order
  • Show all late production orders
  • Show all missing components of a production order
  • Show all late operations of a production order
  • Show all production orders created by <username>
  • Show production orders which satisfy multiple filter criteria including status, type of issue and date filters.

Examples

Now that we went through the pre-requisites and the supported use-cases, let’s see some examples of the use-cases described above. If you have Joule Active and enabled for your user, you should be able to access it in the Fiori Launchpad, by clicking the diamond icon in the right upper-right corner, as highlighted in the Figure below:

Figure 1: Accessing Joule in the Fiori Launchpad

 

We can start with a basic prompt, asking Joule to show all Production Orders for material SG224. As we can see in Figure 2, system shows a list of production orders for our material, and shows the button Open for each order.

Figure 2:  List of production orders in Joule.


If we click button Open, we will branch into the app Manage Production Orders, where we will be able to take actions for those production orders, as shown in Figure 3.

Figure 3: Manage Production Orders app

 

Even inside the app, we can still use Joule, and we can ask for details about a specific Production Order, as we can see in Figure 4.

Figure 4: Details of a Production Order


We can keep asking questions about this order, like the details about the order items and operations, and Joule will understand the context, even if we do not mention the order number again. In Figure 5 we can see how Joule answers when we ask about the operations of the order previously displayed.

  Figure 5: List of Production Order operations

 

Finally, Joule is also capable of answering more complex questions, and show production orders filtering by a specific status and plant, for example, as we can see in Figure 6.

Figure 6: List of partially confirmed Production Orders.

Similar use cases are available for Process Orders, and a comprehensive list of the available use cases with example can be found in the links below:

  • Production Order
  • Process Order

 

More information:

  • List of release specific Joule capabilities: https://help.sap.com/docs/joule/capabilities-guide/release-specific-capabilities
  • Joule Integration SAP Help: https://help.sap.com/docs/joule/integrating-joule-with-sap/integration-with-sap-s-4hana-cloud-private-edition
  • AI Functionality in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition: SAP note 3558794

 

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Regional Implementation Group

entry.title: Re: Total Compensation Plan Template
entry.content: No you cannot use HBA in a Total Compensation template. You can continue to use a standard hierarchy or you can select the rollup or custom hierarchy but it must be the same for all tabs on the template regardless if it is compensation, equity or variable pay. This is available under Define Planners. You must also have the same route map for all tabs of your template.

entry.title: How to prevent sales orders released from credit block to get blocked by BOP
entry.content:
Hello,

In my scenario, when a sales order is created, it may be blocked by Credit Management and it needs to be approved.

However, when a job runs, the BOP (Backorder Processing), it triggers the credit check again, and the document that was already released is blocked again . As a result, a sales order that was already blocked and approved becomes blocked once more. This means the sales order must be approved every time the BOP workflow runs.

Is there a way to prevent that these orders that were already released get blocked again?

Thanks.

entry.title: Re: How to prevent sales orders released from credit bloc...
entry.content:
Hello,

To prevent that these released sales orders to get blocked again, use an exclusion filter.
For the BOP Segment used in your BOP variant, add the exclusion condition:

  • Overall Status of Credit Checks IS Released (D)

If the criteria "Overall Status of Credit Check" is missing in the BOP segment, add this field via the 'Manage Characteristic Catalogs' (F3829) app.

The documentation bellow can also be helpful:

  • How to setup Backorder Processing (BOP) in a dynamic business environment | SAP Community
  • 3282221 - How to exclude credit blocked sales orders from AATP BOP

Best regards.
